episodes.title: Cut to the Chase! The silver bullet
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Standard Chartered's Daniel Lam addresses client questions on whether Dollar-Yen has peaked, with USDJPY at its highest level since 1986. The core debate is whether the Bank of Japan will intervene to defend the yen, and at what level, as the currency's weakness feeds back into accelerating domestic inflation.

**Key points**
- USDJPY has hit its highest level since 1986; verbal intervention from both the BOJ and the US Treasury has so far been ineffective.
- Clients previously flagged 161 as a key intervention level, but Lam believes that level is losing effectiveness, with the BOJ's likely line of defense shifting to 165-166.
- Bear-steepening of the JGB curve signals the market expects the BOJ to stay easy.
- June Tokyo CPI (a leading indicator for national inflation) beat expectations, with higher energy prices spreading into non-energy goods.
- Japan's growth remains resilient, helped by large energy reserves cushioning the energy crisis.
- Market-based inflation expectations remain within the BOJ's tolerance range but are rising quickly.
- Despite firming inflation and growth, markets price only about two BOJ hikes over the next 12 months, pushing meaningful tightening beyond that horizon.
- Core inflation is still below 2%, partly due to government energy subsidies, but BOJ's own forecasts see it rising above 2%, making continued inaction harder to justify.

**FX / rates implications**
- USDJPY trend remains a key regional risk-sentiment driver; a BOJ intervention (verbal or actual) near 165-166 could trigger broad USD-Asia unwind and yen-funded carry-trade unwind, impacting high-yielders like INR, IDR, PHP.
- A weaker yen exacerbates Japanese inflation, creating pressure for eventual BOJ tightening, which if realized would compress the JPY funding carry that has supported EM Asia FX positioning.
- JGB curve bear-steepening reflects rising term premia expectations; any shift in BOJ stance could ripple into global bond yields and risk appetite.

**Watch list**
- BOJ rhetoric/action around the 165-166 USDJPY level as the next intervention threshold.
- Upcoming Tokyo and national CPI prints for signs of inflation momentum broadening beyond energy.
- BOJ policy meetings over the next 6-12 months for signs of a shift away from staying behind the curve.

episodes.title: We're in a renter's market (believe it or not)
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
The episode covers the US rental market shifting toward renters, driven by an apartment construction boom that has outpaced demand. This is a US domestic consumer/housing story with limited direct EM FX/rates transmission, though it touches on the broader US wage-inflation dynamic relevant to Fed policy watchers.

**Key points**
- US asking rent up ~37% since pre-pandemic (Zillow), but only ~2% year-over-year in May 2026 — slower than CPI inflation.
- 2024 saw over 600,000 new apartment units built, the most in decades, concentrated in Sunbelt cities (Nashville, Phoenix, Austin) with looser land-use regulation.
- Nearly half of Zillow-listed rentals in April offered financial move-in incentives (free rent months, waived fees) — a record; in Nashville, some landlords offered up to 3.5 months free.
- Wages grew faster than rent, adding an estimated $2,300/year in extra disposable income for the average renter.
- Market is highly localized: Chicago rent grew 5.5% year-over-year with only ~20% of listings offering incentives, versus abundant Sunbelt supply gluts.
- Congress passed a bipartisan housing supply bill (deregulation to spur construction); Trump has not yet signed but is expected to let it become law without signature, prioritizing his separate election security bill first.
- Homeownership costs run ~37% more monthly than renting (LendingTree).

**FX / rates implications**
- Marginal read for US: rent disinflation (a large CPI shelter component) easing while wage growth outpaces it supports a soft-landing narrative for the Fed, a mild dollar-negative/UST-supportive datapoint if it persists into official CPI shelter prints.
- No direct EM Asia or LATAM currency/rates linkage; relevant mainly as background color on US inflation composition.

**Watch list**
- US CPI shelter/OER component for confirmation that rent deceleration is feeding through to headline inflation.
- Whether Trump signs the bipartisan housing supply bill into law in the coming week.

episodes.title: Credit Crunch: Investor Survey 3Q - Rates, IG vs. HY, US vs. EU
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Bloomberg Intelligence's quarterly Credit Investor Survey shows a sharp sentiment swing from bearish (end-Q1) to bullish (end-Q2) on US/European high-yield and investment-grade credit, discussed with M&G high-yield PM David Fancourt. While primarily a corporate-credit call, the drivers cited (Fed/ECB rate-hike odds, geopolitics, risk appetite) are the same global variables that transmit into EM FX and rates.

**Key points**
- Survey flipped to net positive return expectations for Q3; high yield YTD total return 1.83%, Q2 alone 3.4% (Q1 was -1.5%); starting HY yield 5.3%, breakeven cushion ~200bp/year.
- Positioning swung from net underweight to +10% net overweight credit; valuations still seen as rich (-0.33 std dev), tightest 10% of 20-year spread range for both BB and B credits.
- Rate-hike probabilities for both Fed and ECB have compressed to roughly one hike each for the rest of the year, partly because Middle East/Iran geopolitical risk cooled hawkish pricing.
- Cash levels dropped to 5% (from 5.6%), still above the historical low of 4.5% — read as a contrarian bullish signal.
- Default forecast at 2.4% (survey) vs BI's own base case of ~1.5%/worst-case 2.8-2.9%; index-eligible HY supply forecast raised to ~15bn, though BI/guest see upside past 20bn given a near-zero refinancing premium.
- Big rotation: IG preferred over HY (+32% net vs -16% prior quarter); US HY preferred over European HY narrowed to -14 from -34.

**FX / rates implications**
- Fed/ECB hike-probability repricing (down to ~1 hike each) and Middle East de-escalation are the read-through channels to broad risk sentiment, USD and UST yields relevant for EM carry trades.
- Compressed refinancing premium and heavy anticipated HY/IG supply point to still-benign global credit/liquidity backdrop supportive of EM risk appetite.

**Watch list**
- Fed/ECB rate path for remainder of 2026 (currently priced ~1 hike each).
- Q3 index-eligible HY supply (survey: ~15bn; guest view: >20bn) as a liquidity/technical signal.
- Default rate trajectory (BI 2026 base case ~1.5%) versus survey's more bearish 2.4% forecast.
